content.plain_english: This week in Year 2 maths, your child is working on Money. The learning focuses on: solve problems with addition and subtraction:; using concrete objects and pictorial representations, including those involving numbers, quantities and measures; and applying increasing knowledge of mental and written methods. These skills build directly on what they have already mastered and prepare them for the next steps in the programme of study.
content.in_school: Children use practical measuring equipment — rulers, scales, measuring cylinders, and clocks — to explore measurement in real contexts. They record findings using diagrams and number lines before progressing to written calculations. Problem-solving tasks show how these skills are used every day.
content.home_activity: Give your child a small collection of coins and ask them to make different amounts — can they make 37p in two different ways? At the shops (or in a pretend shop at home), let them work out change from £1 or £2. Handling real money builds confidence quickly.
dinner_table_questions: Can you show me how to solve problems with addition and subtraction:?
Was it easy or tricky to using concrete objects and pictorial representations, including those involving numbers, quantities and measures? Why?
How would you explain today's maths to someone who has never seen it before?

key_vocabulary.definition: The smaller unit of British money — 100p = £1.
The main unit of British money.
The money you get back when you pay more than something costs.
The overall amount when prices are added together.
The dot in a money amount separating pounds from pence — e.g. £2.50.

example_questions: Write three hundred and twelve in numerals.
content.times_table_tip: Year 2 children learn their 2, 5, 10, and 3 times tables. The 3 times table is the new one this year. Try chanting together: 3, 6, 9, 12, 15 … Clapping a beat as you say the numbers helps the rhythm stick.
